news_summary: NASA successfully initiates a mission to rescue a malfunctioning space telescope, showcasing advancements in space technology and problem-solving.
news_summary_fr: La NASA a lancé avec succès une mission visant à secourir un télescope spatial en panne, mettant ainsi en avant les progrès réalisés en matière de technologie spatiale et de résolution de problèmes.
news_summary_es: La NASA pone en marcha con éxito una misión para rescatar un telescopio espacial averiado, lo que pone de manifiesto los avances en tecnología espacial y en la resolución de problemas.

articles.title: Nasa launches mission to save falling space telescope
articles.summary: A Nasa-funded robot has blasted off to catch a falling telescope in mid-orbit and blast it back to safety before it burns up.
articles.content:
The LINK spacecraft, which Lee's team came up with, is a three-armed robot, about the size of a fridge, bristling with cameras and guidance systems and driven by small thrusters.

Launched on Friday, the spacecraft will spend the next few weeks waking up its systems one-by-one: power, navigation, the cameras and sensors it will rely on, and check that each one survived the ride.

Although the Pegasus XL rocket on which it rode has flung LINK close to Swift's orbit, there is still much work for the three-armed robot to do to get close to Swift - the observatory's altitude is shifting week-by-week.

The rescue spacecraft, itself moving, has to home in on a moving target. But about three to four weeks after launch it should finally draw alongside.

Using its cameras and sensors, LINK will slide in close and circle the telescope, photographing it from every angle. Engineers have guessed at where to grab hold, but Swift must have been altered by twenty years in orbit, according to Barber.

"The Swift telescope was never designed to be caught in space and have its orbit changed. So, the rescue craft is going to approach it very slowly and attach itself to the telescope."

Then comes the nail-biting moment: the catch, when LINK's three arms reach out.

If all goes to plan, LINK will take hold of Swift and take it back to where it can continue its vital work.

"LINK will fire its engines to slowly raise the orbit of the telescope again to an altitude where it becomes stable for a long period of time," said Barber.

"It will be a very slow, graceful lift, not a sudden boost to a higher orbit."

Over the following two to three months, LINK will fire its small thrusters and gently haul the pair back uphill, from around 220 miles (360 km) towards Swift's old home 373 miles (600 km) above the Earth.

The mission is ambitious and has never been carried out before. A lot will have to go right if it is to succeed. If it does, attention will turn to whether the next rescue mission could be to save the even more famous Hubble Space Telescope.

Stay tuned.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a pioneering NASA mission with a robotic spacecraft designed to rescue a valuable space telescope by stabilizing its orbit, which represents a significant technological and scientific achievement with broad implications for space exploration and satellite longevity.
articles.personality_title: NASA sends robot to save drifting space telescope
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Swift telescope, an important space observatory, is slowly losing its orbit and risks falling back to Earth where it would burn up. This telescope was not built to be repaired or moved once in space.

**What happened** – NASA launched a robot spacecraft called LINK, which has three arms and many cameras, to catch Swift while both are moving in orbit. LINK was sent into space on a Pegasus XL rocket and is now waking up its systems and preparing to approach the telescope.

**Impact** – This mission is the first time a robot will try to catch and move a space telescope to a safer orbit. LINK will carefully grab the telescope and use its engines to slowly lift it back to a higher, stable orbit so Swift can keep working. This shows new technology that can help save valuable space equipment.

**What's next step** – Over the next two to three months, LINK will gently pull Swift back to its original orbit about 600 kilometers above Earth. If this mission works, NASA might try to rescue other important telescopes like the famous Hubble in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new robot spacecraft is attempting a first-ever rescue to save a falling space telescope by carefully catching and raising it to a safe orbit.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA envoie un robot pour sauver un télescope spatial errant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le télescope Swift, un observatoire spatial important, perd lentement son orbite et risque de retomber sur Terre où il brûlerait. Ce télescope n’a pas été conçu pour être réparé ou déplacé dans l’espace.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a lancé un robot spatial appelé LINK, qui possède trois bras et plusieurs caméras, pour attraper Swift alors que les deux se déplacent en orbite. LINK a été envoyé dans l’espace par une fusée Pegasus XL et prépare maintenant ses systèmes pour s’approcher du télescope.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un robot tente d’attraper et de déplacer un télescope spatial. LINK saisira doucement le télescope et utilisera ses moteurs pour le remonter lentement vers une orbite stable afin que Swift puisse continuer à fonctionner. Cela montre une nouvelle technologie pour sauver des équipements précieux dans l’espace.

**Prochaine étape** – Dans les deux à trois mois à venir, LINK tirera doucement Swift vers son orbite d’origine, à environ 600 kilomètres au-dessus de la Terre. Si la mission réussit, la NASA pourrait essayer de sauver d’autres télescopes importants comme le célèbre Hubble.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau robot spatial de la NASA tente un sauvetage inédit pour attraper et remonter un télescope spatial en chute vers une orbite sécurisée.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA envía un robot para salvar un telescopio espacial que se desplaza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El telescopio Swift, un importante observatorio espacial, está perdiendo su órbita poco a poco y corre el riesgo de caer a la Tierra donde se quemaría. Este telescopio no fue diseñado para ser reparado o movido en el espacio.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA lanzó una nave robot llamada LINK, que tiene tres brazos y muchas cámaras, para atrapar a Swift mientras ambos se mueven en órbita. LINK fue enviado al espacio en un cohete Pegasus XL y ahora está activando sus sistemas para acercarse al telescopio.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que un robot intenta atrapar y mover un telescopio espacial. LINK sujetará con cuidado al telescopio y usará sus motores para elevarlo lentamente a una órbita estable para que Swift siga funcionando. Esto muestra nueva tecnología que puede ayudar a salvar equipos valiosos en el espacio.

**Próximo paso** – Durante los próximos dos o tres meses, LINK tirará suavemente de Swift para devolverlo a su órbita original, a unos 600 kilómetros sobre la Tierra. Si la misión tiene éxito, la NASA podría intentar rescatar otros telescopios importantes como el famoso Hubble.

**Resumen en una frase** – La nueva nave robot de la NASA intenta un rescate sin precedentes para salvar un telescopio espacial en caída al atraparlo y elevarlo a una órbita segura.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a three-armed robotic spacecraft gently reaching out to embrace a large, weathered space telescope drifting above a softly glowing Earth horizon, both illuminated by warm, natural tones of twilight and surrounded by the calm vastness of space.
